chunk_summary: This section addresses drooling management in neurodisability, emphasizing secretion aspiration prevention through non-pharmacologic and pharmacologic interventions, including glycopyrrolate dosing and botulinum toxin escalation for severe cases.
learning_objectives: Differentiate anterior drooling from posterior drooling and assess associated risks.
Implement non-pharmacologic strategies for drooling management, including oral hygiene and posture optimization.
Understand glycopyrrolate dosing guidelines and monitor for neurodisability-specific cautions.
Recognize indications for botulinum toxin injections and surgical interventions in refractory cases.
key_facts: Posterior drooling is associated with higher risks, including recurrent chest infections and sleep desaturation.
Non-pharmacologic measures include twice-daily oral hygiene with fluoride toothpaste and chlorhexidine support.
Glycopyrrolate pediatric dosing: 0.02 mg/kg per dose three times daily, titrated every 5–7 days to a maximum of 0.1 mg/kg per dose.
Botulinum toxin injections are indicated for health harm or major participation restrictions despite optimized therapy.
Surgical interventions are reserved for severe refractory cases and must consider swallow safety.
reference_quote: Glycopyrrolate has labeled pediatric dosing for chronic drooling in neurologic conditions (commonly ages 3–16 years).
document_key_topics: Escalation to botulinum toxin or surgery for severe cases
